description: Implement granular dev reload for the Chrome extension: side panel UI changes trigger a lightweight page refresh (window.location.reload) instead of a full chrome.runtime.reload(), while background/offscreen changes still trigger a full extension reload. Uses a WebSocket relay server embedded in the dev orchestrator to signal the extension, with ID-based targeting so each build target only reloads what it needs.

userStories.title: Add dev reload WebSocket relay server to the dev orchestrator
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the dev orchestrator to run a lightweight WebSocket relay server so that built extension artifacts can signal the running extension to reload granularly.
acceptanceCriteria: A WebSocket server starts on a configurable port (default 18515) when `npm run dev` launches
The server accepts connections from extension clients and build signal senders
When the server receives a BUILD_COMPLETE message with an `id` field, it broadcasts a DO_UPDATE message with the same `id` to all connected clients
The server handles port-in-use errors gracefully (logs a warning, disables dev reload signaling, does not crash the orchestrator)
The server shuts down cleanly on SIGINT/SIGTERM with the rest of the orchestrator
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes: Create a new file `scripts/dev-reload-server.ts` that exports a `startDevReloadServer(port: number)` function returning `{ broadcast(id: string): void; close(): void }`. The WebSocket server should use the `ws` package (already a dependency). Message protocol: `{ type: 'build_complete', id: string }` inbound from build, `{ type: 'do_update', id: string }` outbound to clients. Keep it simple — no authentication needed since this is localhost-only dev tooling. The relay server is imported and started in `scripts/dev.ts` right before the startup banner (after initial build, before tsc recompile watcher). Add cleanup to the existing `cleanup()` function. Use a non-standard port (18515) to avoid conflicts with the MCP server (9515). The port should be exported as a constant so the extension can reference it at build time.

userStories.title: Split extension rebuild into targeted build functions
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the dev orchestrator to distinguish between side panel changes and background/offscreen changes so that it can rebuild only what changed.
acceptanceCriteria: The orchestrator's `buildExtension()` function is refactored into separate `buildSidePanel()` and `buildBackground()` functions
A new `determineChangedTargets()` function analyzes which tsc output files changed by comparing mtimes before and after compilation
When only side-panel source files changed, only `buildSidePanel()` + install runs
When only background/offscreen source files changed, only `buildBackground()` + install runs
When files from both categories (or shared dependencies) changed, both builds run
After each targeted build, the orchestrator calls `broadcast(id)` on the relay server with the appropriate target ID ('side-panel' for UI-only, 'extension' for background/offscreen/both)
The existing full `buildExtension()` is preserved for the initial build (before the relay server is needed)
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Modify `scripts/dev.ts`. The change detection strategy: before each tsc compilation, snapshot mtimes of key dist/ files. After compilation, compare. The key insight is which *source* directories changed — map dist output paths back to source:

- `platform/browser-extension/dist/side-panel/` → side panel only
- `platform/browser-extension/dist/background.js` or `platform/browser-extension/dist/offscreen/` → background/offscreen
- `platform/browser-extension/dist/` (other files like bridge, messaging, etc.) → shared code used by both → rebuild both
- Any other platform package dist/ change (mcp-server, plugin-sdk, shared, cli) → not extension code, skip extension rebuild

A simpler approach that's more robust: use tsc's own output. After tsc finishes, scan `platform/browser-extension/dist/` for files with mtime newer than the compilation start timestamp. Categorize them:
- Files under `dist/side-panel/` → side panel target
- `dist/background.js`, files under `dist/offscreen/` → background target
- All other `.js` files in `dist/` (e.g., `dist/bridge.js`, `dist/messaging.js`, `dist/message-router.js`) → shared, triggers both targets

The `scheduleExtensionRebuild` callback should be updated to call this analysis, then conditionally run only the needed build steps. After build + install, call `reloadServer.broadcast('side-panel')` or `reloadServer.broadcast('extension')` instead of calling the old `reloadExtension()` POST endpoint.

Keep the old `reloadExtension()` function as a fallback — if the WebSocket relay is not running (e.g., port conflict), fall back to the full `POST /extension/reload` behavior.

userStories.title: Inject dev reload client into the side panel bundle
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the side panel to automatically connect to the dev reload WebSocket server and refresh itself when it receives a matching update signal, so that UI changes take effect without a full extension restart.
acceptanceCriteria: In dev builds, a dev reload client script is prepended to the side panel bundle
The client connects to ws://localhost:18515 and listens for DO_UPDATE messages with id 'side-panel'
On receiving a matching DO_UPDATE, the client calls window.location.reload() to refresh the side panel page
The client does NOT trigger chrome.runtime.reload() — only a page-level refresh
The client reconnects automatically if the WebSocket disconnects (with exponential backoff)
In production builds (npm run build), the dev reload client is NOT included
The side panel correctly refreshes on a UI-only change without losing the background script's WebSocket connection to the MCP server
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Modify `platform/browser-extension/build-side-panel.ts` to accept a dev mode flag. The approach:

1. Create `platform/browser-extension/src/dev-reload-client.ts` containing the WebSocket client code. This file is a self-contained IIFE that:
   - Connects to `ws://localhost:${DEV_RELOAD_PORT}`
   - Listens for `{ type: 'do_update', id: string }` messages
   - If `id === 'side-panel'` OR `id === 'extension'`, calls `window.location.reload()`
   - On close/error, reconnects with exponential backoff (500ms → 1s → 2s → 4s, cap 10s)
   - Logs connection status to console with `[dev-reload]` prefix

2. In `build-side-panel.ts`, check for `process.env.OPENTABS_DEV` (set by the dev orchestrator). If truthy, read the dev reload client source file and use an esbuild `banner` option to prepend it to the output bundle, wrapped in an IIFE. The banner approach means no import needed — it's just prepended JS text.

3. The `DEV_RELOAD_PORT` constant should be imported from a shared location. Since this is a build-time concern, define it in a file that both `scripts/dev-reload-server.ts` and the build script can import. A good location is `scripts/dev-reload-constants.ts` exporting `DEV_RELOAD_PORT = 18515`. The build script can use `define` in esbuild to replace a `__DEV_RELOAD_PORT__` global with the actual port value.

4. The dev reload client should respond to BOTH 'side-panel' and 'extension' IDs — because if the background changes, the extension does a full reload which destroys the side panel anyway, but if the side panel receives the signal first it can refresh faster. This is harmless.

5. The client must work within MV3 CSP. WebSocket connections from chrome-extension:// pages are allowed by default CSP (no connect-src restriction). The script is bundled as part of the extension's own JS — satisfies script-src 'self'. No CSP modification needed.

Dev mode detection: `scripts/dev.ts` should set `OPENTABS_DEV=1` in the environment before calling build functions, or pass it as an argument to `buildSidePanel()`. The simpler approach is to pass `--dev` as a CLI arg to the build script.

userStories.title: Inject dev reload client into the background bundle for full extension reload
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the background service worker to listen for dev reload signals that indicate background/offscreen code changed, and trigger a full chrome.runtime.reload() — replacing the current POST /extension/reload HTTP mechanism with a direct WebSocket signal.
acceptanceCriteria: In dev builds, a dev reload client script is prepended to the background bundle
The client connects to ws://localhost:18515 and listens for DO_UPDATE messages with id 'extension'
On receiving a matching DO_UPDATE, the client calls chrome.runtime.reload()
The client does NOT react to 'side-panel' updates — only 'extension' triggers a full reload
In production builds, the dev reload client is NOT included
The dev orchestrator no longer calls POST /extension/reload for background changes — the WebSocket signal replaces it
The existing POST /extension/reload endpoint on the MCP server is preserved (used by production and opentabs-plugin build) but the dev orchestrator no longer uses it
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Modify `platform/browser-extension/build-extension.ts` to accept a dev mode flag, similar to US-003.

1. Create `platform/browser-extension/src/dev-reload-background.ts` containing the background-specific WebSocket client. This is different from the side panel client because:
   - It only responds to `id === 'extension'` (not 'side-panel')
   - It calls `chrome.runtime.reload()` instead of `window.location.reload()`
   - Before reloading, it should clear `wsConnected` from session storage (same as the existing `handleExtensionReload` in `message-router.ts:232-251`) to prevent stale state on restart
   - It runs in a service worker context (no `window`, no `document`)

2. In `build-extension.ts`, check for `process.env.OPENTABS_DEV`. If truthy, prepend the dev reload background client to the background bundle output using esbuild's `banner` option. Only prepend to `background.js`, NOT to `offscreen/index.js` — the offscreen document doesn't need its own reload client since it's destroyed when the extension reloads.

3. In `scripts/dev.ts`, update `scheduleExtensionRebuild` to:
   - For background/offscreen changes: call `reloadServer.broadcast('extension')` — the background script's embedded client handles the rest
   - For side-panel-only changes: call `reloadServer.broadcast('side-panel')` — the side panel's embedded client handles the rest
   - Remove the `reloadExtension()` POST call from the rebuild pipeline (keep the function for fallback if relay server is unavailable)

4. The service worker WebSocket client needs to handle the service worker lifecycle. Service workers can be terminated by Chrome when idle. On `do_update`, the reload is immediate so this is not a concern. For the WebSocket connection itself: if it disconnects, the reconnect logic keeps it alive. Chrome's alarm API can be used as a keepalive if needed, but in practice during active dev the service worker stays alive due to the side panel being open.

5. Important: the `WS_CONNECTED_KEY` constant is defined in `platform/browser-extension/src/constants.ts`. The dev reload background script needs this value. Since it's prepended as a banner (not imported), hardcode the key string 'wsConnected' in the dev reload script (it's a stable constant that won't change).

userStories.title: Add dev mode environment variable plumbing to extension builds
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the dev orchestrator to pass a dev mode flag to extension build scripts so they know whether to include the dev reload clients.
acceptanceCriteria: The dev orchestrator sets OPENTABS_DEV=1 environment variable before running extension build commands
build-side-panel.ts reads OPENTABS_DEV and conditionally includes the dev reload client
build-extension.ts reads OPENTABS_DEV and conditionally includes the dev reload client in the background bundle
npm run build (production) does NOT set OPENTABS_DEV, so no dev reload clients are included
The install-extension.ts script copies the dev-built artifacts correctly
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
This story wires the plumbing between the orchestrator and the build scripts.

In `scripts/dev.ts`:
- When calling `buildExtension()`, `buildSidePanel()`, or `buildBackground()`, set `process.env.OPENTABS_DEV = '1'` before spawning the child processes. Since `runWithPrefix` uses `spawn`, the child processes inherit the parent's environment. Alternatively, pass it explicitly in the spawn env.
- Actually, the simpler approach: since `buildExtension` calls `npm run build:bundle` and `npm run build:side-panel` via `runWithPrefix`, and those spawn child processes that inherit `process.env`, setting `process.env.OPENTABS_DEV = '1'` at the top of dev.ts (before any builds) is sufficient.

In `build-side-panel.ts`:
- Read `const isDev = process.env.OPENTABS_DEV === '1'`
- If isDev, read the dev reload client source, bundle it with esbuild separately (or inline it), and prepend via the `banner` esbuild option
- Use esbuild's `define` to replace `__DEV_RELOAD_PORT__` with the actual port number in the dev reload client

In `build-extension.ts`:
- Same pattern but for the background bundle only
- The banner should only apply to the background entry point, not offscreen

Note: The dev reload client TypeScript files (`src/dev-reload-client.ts` and `src/dev-reload-background.ts`) from US-003/US-004 need to be compiled to JS before they can be used as banner text. Two options:
  a) Write them as plain JS files (no compilation needed) — simpler, no build step dependency
  b) Use esbuild to compile them inline before using as banner text

Option (a) is recommended for simplicity. Place them in `platform/browser-extension/src/dev/reload-client.js` and `src/dev/reload-background.js` as plain JS. Read them with `readFileSync` in the build scripts.

userStories.title: Add dev reload status to the startup banner and console output
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want clear visual feedback in the dev console about what kind of reload is happening so I can understand the dev tool behavior at a glance.
acceptanceCriteria: The startup banner includes the dev reload WebSocket port (e.g., 'Dev Reload: ws://localhost:18515')
When a side-panel-only rebuild triggers, the console shows '[ext] Side panel refreshed (UI-only)'
When a background rebuild triggers, the console shows '[ext] Extension reloaded (full)'
When a mixed rebuild triggers, the console shows '[ext] Extension reloaded (full — background + UI changed)'
When the dev reload server fails to start (port in use), the banner shows 'Dev Reload: disabled (port 18515 in use)' and fallback to POST /extension/reload is used
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Modify `scripts/dev.ts`.

Update the startup banner `contentLines` array to include the dev reload port info. The banner is built at lines 319-336.

Update the log messages in the rebuild pipeline:
- After `buildSidePanel()` succeeds + broadcast('side-panel'): log `[ext] Side panel refreshed (UI-only)`
- After `buildBackground()` succeeds + broadcast('extension'): log `[ext] Extension reloaded (full)`
- After both build + broadcast('extension'): log `[ext] Extension reloaded (full — background + UI changed)`

The fallback path (when relay server is unavailable) should log `[ext] Extension reloaded (full — via HTTP fallback)`.

This is the final story and touches browser-observable behavior (the extension reload mechanism changes), so mark as e2eCheckpoint.
